accepted_028.assessment:
This paper presents a neat and practical idea—rotating temporal attention maps to flip the motion-time association—and shows that, with minimal fine-tuning focused on Wv/Wo, a standard image-to-video model can be adapted for keyframe interpolation. The synchronized dual-directional sampling is a reasonable and effective mechanism to reduce forward/backward inconsistencies. The empirical results are compelling against FILM/TRF/DynamiCrafter in the targeted long-gap regime, and the ablations support the core design choices. However, the manuscript currently lacks crucial implementation details on how the rotated attention is injected, overstates the guarantee of motion opposition, omits several closely related and timely baselines, and does not report compute/runtime. These issues are fixable. With clarifications, stronger baselines (particularly ViBiDSampler and timestamp-aware approaches), and additional interpolation-specific metrics, the paper would make a solid and useful contribution to generative video interpolation at ICLR. I lean toward acceptance if these concerns are addressed.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_052.assessment:
This paper offers a pragmatic and well-executed approach to unifying context-length extension with efficient long-context serving through a simple, GPU-friendly hybrid architecture. The methodological novelty is moderate—static sparse patterns and hybrid architectures are known—but the integration, careful kernel design, and strong empirical ablations make it a valuable contribution. The results convincingly show that a small amount of post-training (5B tokens) suffices to reach near–full-attention quality on retrieval and competitive long-context reasoning while delivering substantial efficiency gains. The ablation that full attention should be concentrated in middle layers is particularly insightful and actionable.
The main areas that temper enthusiasm are limited breadth in long-context evaluation (RULER restricted to two QA tasks, omission of more variable but still relevant tasks) and incomplete comparisons against the strongest recent runtime compression/offload systems. Nevertheless, the work’s simplicity, robustness across two model scales, and real speed/memory improvements make it likely to be useful to the community. I lean toward acceptance, especially if the camera-ready can (i) broaden the evaluation (batch-level serving, additional long-context tasks), (ii) strengthen baseline tuning, and (iii) clarify/release kernel and integration details.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_058.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and timely problem—quantifying retrieval’s contribution to RAG—with a conceptually appealing semantic, sampling-based perspective. The empirical results are promising, suggesting better alignment with human utility judgments than common baselines and offering useful diagnostic insights across RAG components. However, there is a fundamental concern with the estimator’s correctness: summing model likelihoods over samples drawn from the same distribution does not yield an unbiased estimator of probability mass, calling into question the probabilistic interpretation and possibly the magnitude of reported gains. Additionally, the definition of “Semantic Perplexity” is inconsistent with the implemented quantity, and several experimental choices (uniform multi-hop utility, absent comparisons to key baselines, limited sensitivity analyses) limit the strength of the claims. I believe the paper could become a strong contribution with a corrected estimator, clarified definition/naming, broader baselines, and deeper ablations. As it stands, I recommend a borderline reject, primarily due to the estimator issue and definitional inconsistencies; with revisions addressing these points, the work would be of clear interest to the ICLR community working on RAG evaluation and model-based measurement.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_022.assessment:
This paper delivers a well-engineered, practical contribution: a scalable pipeline to synthesize multimodal tool-usage trajectories and a demonstration that supervised trajectory tuning substantially improves VLM-driven agents on GTA and GAIA. The focus on multi-file inputs and a broad toolset is timely and useful, and the reported gains across two backbones are compelling. However, the work remains primarily an engineering/data contribution with limited methodological novelty; important reproducibility details (tool backends, execution environment) and deeper ablations are missing; and there are legitimate concerns about circularity from using the same LLM family for generation and verification. I view the paper as borderline to weak accept, with the recommendation strengthened if the authors (i) release MM-Traj, tool APIs, and code with clear licensing; (ii) clarify the training objective and tool backends; and (iii) add more rigorous ablations and leakage checks. Given the demonstrated improvements and potential value of the dataset to the community, I lean positive, contingent on these clarifications.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_064.assessment:
TetSphere Splatting presents a thoughtfully engineered Lagrangian representation that prioritizes mesh quality via volumetric structure and principled regularization. The idea of deformable tetrahedral spheres is compelling, and the results demonstrate substantial gains in triangle quality, manifoldness, and structural integrity with competitive reconstruction accuracy. The work addresses an important and under-emphasized dimension—mesh usability—making it valuable for downstream rendering and simulation. However, aspects of the methodology (injectivity guarantees, Laplacian discretization) and parts of the evaluation (MR definition for open surfaces, missing ablations on M/N/T, largely qualitative generative results) need clarification and strengthening. Some claims (paracompactness/topology) are overstated and should be tempered. Overall, I view this as a strong systems/representation paper with clear practical impact and moderate novelty; with the clarifications and added ablations suggested above, it would be a solid contribution to ICLR.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_070.assessment:
This paper presents a compelling and largely coherent framework for understanding architecture-dependent inductive biases in input space via average geometry and its evolution. The central claim—early training changes align with a projection of data covariance onto an architecture-determined geometry (Δ ≈ G S G)—is theoretically justified for simplified models and empirically supported across multiple architectures, and the proposed Geometric Invariance Hypothesis nicely explains when and why generalization can fail even for simple discriminants. The work innovatively extends prior empirical observations (e.g., NADs) into a more general and interpretable picture, with thoughtful applications. That said, the main invariance result remains conjectural, and several experimental choices (removal of normalization layers, reliance on Gaussian probes, stylized synthetic setups) limit immediate applicability. Some presentation issues (notational typos, reliance on appendices for key derivations) and missing comparisons (e.g., to Fisher-based baselines, alternative subspace metrics, standard recipes) also temper the strength of the evidence. Overall, I view this as a valuable contribution that advances our understanding of inductive biases in input space, with room for tightening theory and strengthening empirical breadth. I lean toward acceptance after addressing clarity and adding a few robustness checks.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_076.assessment:
This paper offers a substantial and timely advance in the theory of causal reasoning by aligning computational complexity with Pearl’s causal hierarchy in the presence of succinct marginalization. The NP^PP/PSPACE/NEXP completeness results for basic/linear fragments with Σ are technically robust and conceptually clarifying, overturning earlier impressions that higher rungs might not be computationally worse than the probabilistic level. The succ-∃R classification for counterfactual polynomials with Σ closes an open problem and completes a coherent picture across layers. The writing is generally clear though marred by minor typos, and some proof components (particularly the L3 hardness construction and PSPACE space accounting) would benefit from expanded detail in the main text. The work is well-situated relative to foundational literature, with an opportunity to further connect to parameterized frontiers. Overall, I find the contributions original, significant, and likely to influence both theoretical understanding and practical algorithm design in causal reasoning. I recommend acceptance after minor clarifications.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_082.assessment:
UniCBE addresses an important, practical problem—efficient, scalable CBE for rapidly evolving LLM ecosystems—by unifying three intuitively complementary objectives via a simple, implementable policy. The empirical coverage is solid and the ablations are informative; the dynamic model-addition and list-wise results are especially compelling for real-world evaluation settings. However, the theoretical argument for uniform sampling as “optimal” is only loosely connected to the actual CBE setup, the uncertainty modeling is heuristic rather than principled, and the experimental comparison set omits several strong, directly relevant baselines from active ranking and information-theoretic acquisition. Some presentation issues (notation clarity, Δ vs. savings inconsistencies, MT-Bench setup) and a lack of computational analysis further weaken the case. Overall, I view this as a promising, practically useful contribution whose current form is incremental and would benefit from stronger baselines, tighter theory-to-practice connections, clearer reporting, and complexity analysis. I lean toward a borderline rejection at this time but believe a revised version addressing these points could be a valuable addition to the literature.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_088.assessment:
KOR-Bench addresses an important gap: evaluating rule-following and OOD reasoning with minimal reliance on memorized knowledge. The benchmark is thoughtfully constructed across diverse categories and is accompanied by careful analyses (cipher sub-steps, self-correction, complex multi-rule tasks) that yield actionable insights into current model limitations. However, the central orthogonality claim is insufficiently substantiated: contamination and leakage risks are nontrivial given the sources and domains, and the paper does not present strong empirical audits to validate independence from pre-trained knowledge. Methodological choices (asymmetric prompting, single-run reporting without variance/confidence intervals, and regex-centric evaluation with per-rule handling) raise additional concerns about rigor and reproducibility. In my view, the work is promising and potentially valuable to the community, especially if the authors (1) strengthen the orthogonality evidence with concrete audits/ablations, (2) standardize and better document evaluation procedures with multiple runs and CIs, and (3) adopt more programmatic verifiers and distractor-rule settings to disentangle rule selection vs execution. With these improvements, KOR-Bench could become a strong, complementary resource to existing rule-following and logic/puzzle benchmarks. As it stands, I lean toward a weak accept contingent on addressing the above points, given the importance of the problem and the scope of analyses; otherwise, it is a solid borderline submission.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_094.assessment:
The paper tackles an important problem—scaling constructive neural VRP solvers—and proposes a conceptually appealing combination of GFlowNets with adversarial reward shaping and a sparse-graph GNN backbone. The empirical results, especially on larger instances, are promising and the method’s efficiency is potentially valuable. However, the submission currently lacks critical technical details (feasibility handling, backward policy, reward normalization), contains equation/notation inconsistencies, and raises fairness questions in baseline comparisons (notably GFACS being evaluated without its core inference components). It also under-cites or omits highly relevant recent work (e.g., OD-DEAL, ECO) that would help contextualize novelty and contributions. With clarifications, fairer baselines, and stronger methodological transparency, this work could be a solid contribution; in its present form, I find it borderline due to these gaps. I encourage the authors to address the questions above, tighten the technical exposition, and expand comparisons to closely related contemporaneous methods.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_000.assessment:
This paper delivers a substantial and timely empirical contribution to understanding attention sinks in autoregressive LMs. Its central insights—that sinks behave like key-side attention reservoirs, that their position and strength are governed by optimization/data/loss choices, and that removing softmax normalization prevents sink formation up to 1B parameters—are novel and actionable. The experimental scope is broad and well-executed, and the key-bias interventions are especially convincing in clarifying the role of keys versus values. The main limitations are the reliance on thresholded metrics, proxy measurements for non-normalized attention, and a largely empirical (rather than mechanistic) basis for attributing sinks to softmax’s inner dependence. Further validation at larger scales and on more diverse downstream tasks would enhance confidence in eliminating normalization as a general remedy. Overall, the work is of high interest to the ICLR community, both for deepening mechanistic understanding and for guiding practical pretraining/architecture decisions.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_006.assessment:
The paper targets an important and timely problem—reaction-conditioned, function-first enzyme pocket design—and presents a coherent SE(3) flow framework, a plausible hierarchical pretraining strategy, and an ambitious new dataset. Empirical results show consistent structural gains and improved EC-class proxies over selected baselines. However, the most distinctive element—the enzyme–reaction co-evolution via joint MSAs over enzyme sequences and reaction SMILES—lacks biological/statistical justification and rigorous validation. The dataset construction (AlphaFill on AlphaFold models) and evaluation splits raise concerns about correctness and leakage, and binding proxies favor baselines more tailored to binding. Missing comparisons to closely related function-first works (e.g., GENzyme/EnzyGen) weaken positioning and the case for novelty. Overall, while the direction is promising and the engineering effort is substantial, I believe the paper requires significant strengthening in methodological soundness (co-evolution), dataset validation/splits, and functional evaluation before being ready for a top-tier venue. I recommend rejection in the current form, with suggestions above to improve clarity, rigor, and fairness.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_012.assessment:
This work presents a clear and practically useful idea: weight conditional behavioral cloning by how diagnostic each state-action is for the intended style, using an information-theoretic quantity (PMI) as the signal. The objective is simple, theoretically well-motivated via limiting cases, and empirically shows meaningful gains in style calibration and interpretability across multiple domains, including human data. However, there are notable methodological and experimental gaps: the use of MINE without comparison to simpler posterior estimators, the unusual baseline subtraction that can induce negative weights without safeguards, limited ablations, and potentially weakened baselines (InfoGAIL offline, partial SORL). These issues make it difficult to fully assess robustness and generality. Overall, I view this as a promising and relevant contribution with solid empirical indications, but recommend acceptance only if the authors can address the estimator choice and negative-weight concerns and strengthen baselines/ablations. As it stands, I lean slightly positive due to the clarity, simplicity, and demonstrated benefits on style calibration, but the paper is borderline for a top-tier venue.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_018.assessment:
This paper addresses an important and timely problem—scalable, controllable simulation for moving sound sources—and provides a useful toolkit and a sizable dataset with broad baseline evaluations. The empirical results are promising, particularly the improved transfer to real recordings in both separation and enhancement settings, and the environmental-factor study is insightful and consistent with recent literature. However, several claims about acoustic realism and diffraction are stronger than the provided evidence, and the methodology for time-varying rendering (signal interpolation) would benefit from validation and corrections to the presented formula. The comparative evaluations conflate multiple factors (scale, mixing recipe, motion, acoustics), and the lack of multichannel baselines underutilizes the toolkit’s advertised strengths. With clarifications, added controls (data-size/mixing-equalized comparisons, waypoint spacing ablations), richer acoustic metadata, and at least a preliminary multichannel benchmark, this work could be a strong contribution. In its current form, I lean toward acceptance if the authors address the key technical and experimental concerns in the rebuttal and camera-ready; otherwise, it remains a valuable resource paper but falls short of ICLR’s standard for substantiating central claims about realism and generalization.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_024.assessment:
This is an insightful and timely paper that brings fresh, empirically grounded perspective to why scaling laws arise in AlphaZero-like RL and when they break down. By tying Zipf-like state visitation (emerging from game-tree structure) to loss scaling and by diagnosing a data-distribution-driven inverse scaling mechanism in games with converging endgame trees, the paper offers a coherent narrative with concrete evidence. While aspects of the methodology could be more rigorous—particularly power-law fitting, multi-seed robustness, solver-based validation beyond Connect Four, and stronger ablations to rule out compute/target-quality confounds—the core observations are novel and valuable. The paper should stimulate follow-up work on curriculum design and distribution-aware scaling in RL. I lean toward acceptance, contingent on clarifications and, where feasible, additional robustness analyses.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_030.assessment:
This paper presents a conceptually neat and practically appealing advance for online learning in mean field games: treating value and population as a single parameter and updating them via a simple uni-timescale semi-SGD. The introduction of PA-LFA to parameterize population measures in continuous spaces, along with finite-time convergence and approximation-error analyses, is novel and relevant. While the main theoretical guarantees rely on strong but standard assumptions (global Lipschitzness and uniform ergodicity leading to contractivity), the analysis is careful and in line with recent stochastic approximation work. Empirically, the results convincingly support improved stability and efficiency over FPI variants and demonstrate the benefits of PA-LFA over discretization, though comparisons with recent single-loop online baselines and standardized benchmarks would strengthen the case. Overall, I find the paper makes a meaningful contribution with clear potential impact and recommend acceptance, contingent on clarifying assumptions and expanding empirical comparisons where feasible.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_036.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and timely problem—loss of plasticity during long-horizon continual learning—with a method that is both principled and simple. The theoretical analysis of a single ReLU under adversarial targets crisply illustrates why L2 regularization can be insufficient and why resets can be necessary, while the hypothesis-testing perspective provides a clear, neuron-specific rationale for the SNR trigger. The empirical scope (multiple benchmarks and architectures) is appropriate and the reported gains and robustness are promising. The main areas to strengthen are (i) clarifying and standardizing the practical definition of “firing” and the reset operation across modern architectures/activations; (ii) adding comparisons to closely related recent reset/reinit methods (SWR, ReSiN) and quantifying overhead; and (iii) expanding sensitivity and ablation studies (window length, thresholding, effect on forgetting). With these additions, I would view the paper as a strong contribution. As is, I recommend a positive but conditional assessment: the ideas are sound and significant, but the experimental section should be completed with missing baselines and implementation details to meet the bar for a top-tier venue.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_016.assessment:
This paper presents a well-motivated and technically interesting advance for BOED: a pooled-posterior-based EIG gradient estimator combined with a single-loop bi-level optimization scheme that leverages both classical and diffusion-based samplers. The unification and extension of prior EIG-gradient methods is illuminating, the proposal is elegant, and the empirical gains on a standard sequential benchmark are compelling. The diffusion-prior inpainting example is a strong proof of concept for “data-based BOED,” though the empirical section would benefit from stronger baselines and ablations, and the paper lacks variance/convergence analysis of the pooled-importance gradient estimator and the single-loop dynamics. Overall, I view CoDiff as a valuable contribution likely to interest the ICLR community working at the intersection of BOED, sampling, and diffusion models. I recommend acceptance after addressing the questions/ablation gaps and clarifying the design-gradient handling in discrete settings.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_004.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and timely problem: how to construct effective long-context training data that balance semantic coherence and diversity without relying on scarce domain-skewed long documents. Quest is a simple, scalable, and practically useful synthesis procedure that consistently improves long-context performance across model sizes and preserves short-text ability. The empirical gains, especially at 128k and in LLaMA-3-8B applications, suggest tangible value to the community.
That said, the technical novelty is moderate, and key methodological details (particularly for baseline configuration and similarity computation) are incomplete, limiting reproducibility and interpretability. The Needle-in-a-Haystack evaluations mix variants (sentence vs numeric), complicating SOTA claims. The scaling-law section is a minor contribution with limited insight.
Overall, I view Quest as a valuable data-centric contribution with clear practical impact. With stronger baselines/specifications, full release of evaluation protocols, and expanded ablations on its heuristics, this work would meet the bar more convincingly. I lean toward a weak accept given the importance of the problem and the empirical utility demonstrated, contingent on clarifications and artifact release.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_040.assessment:
This paper presents a substantial engineering contribution to the autoformalization ecosystem: a structure-aware deformalization pipeline grounded in Lean-4 compiler artifacts, a large curated NL–FL dataset (statements and proofs), and a strong statement translator with new augmentation strategies. The methodological choices make sense, the dataset appears valuable, and the open release is a clear community good. However, the evaluation raises concerns: unusually high miniF2F numbers hinge on an automated pipeline with known false positives, without clear parity of sampling budgets and protocols for baselines; data leakage and de-duplication are not rigorously analyzed; and key contributions (dependency ordering, retrieval) are not ablated. The section-level Stacks case study is promising but under-quantified and currently shows limited end-to-end automation on proofs.
Overall, I view the work as promising and impactful, with a solid engineering foundation likely to benefit the community. To reach top-tier standards, the authors should strengthen the evaluation rigor (matched budgets, more human audits, overlap analysis), clarify inconsistencies, and provide ablations isolating the key pipeline components. With these addressed, I would be more confident in the claims. As is, I lean toward a weak accept due to the dataset/method contributions and the potential for high impact, while encouraging the authors to shore up the empirical evidence and reporting clarity.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_054.assessment:
The paper addresses an important and underexplored evaluation dimension—super-long, instruction-compliant generation—and offers a pragmatic, interpretable benchmark that is likely to be useful to the community. The scenario and instruction design are sensible, the negative results are informative, and the open release is valuable. However, the current submission leaves critical methodological details unspecified (output parsing/matching, formal metric definitions), mixes token and word length accounting, and contains several presentation inconsistencies that reduce confidence in the reported numbers. Additionally, missing comparisons to closely related recent work on long-form, multi-constraint generation limit the contextualization of contributions. With clarifications to the evaluation pipeline, tighter metric formalization, decoding/length-control ablations, and a small human validation, this work could make a strong contribution. In its current form, I view it as promising but not yet ready for a top-tier venue.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_066.assessment:
This paper addresses an important and timely problem: achieving geometrically accurate, efficient large-scale reconstruction with Gaussian splatting. The proposed combination—SSIM-driven densification, elongation filtering for stability in parallel tuning, and a streamlined pipeline with early SH2, per-block trimming, and quantization—works well in practice and is supported by clear ablations and solid results on challenging datasets. The new TnT-style evaluation protocol with visibility-aware cropping is also a valuable contribution to the community. The primary limitations are moderate originality (several components are straightforward heuristics or engineering refinements), incomplete comparisons to closely related and contemporaneous methods (notably UrbanGS, TrimGS, FastGS, Turbo-GS), and some missing sensitivity analyses and reproducibility details. Overall, the paper provides a useful system-level advance with demonstrable benefits and a promising evaluation protocol. With stronger positioning against recent large-scale geometry-focused baselines and additional robustness analyses, it would make a convincing ICLR contribution.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_072.assessment:
This paper addresses a timely and practically relevant question—how KANs compare to MLPs on irregular and noisy targets—using controlled synthetic 1D experiments with matched parameter counts, varied sample sizes and epochs, and optimizer comparisons. The main qualitative takeaways (KANs excel on smooth and some singular/oscillatory cases; MLPs do better on non-differentiable points and jumps; noise degrades performance for both; KANs are more compute-intensive) are plausible and useful. However, the study falls short of ICLR standards due to limited novelty (1D regression without new methodology), insufficient experimental rigor (single runs, partial fairness—epochs rather than time, optimizer asymmetries), under-specified protocols (noise models, splits, losses), missing comparisons to relevant KAN/MLP variants, and internal inconsistencies (notably contradictory claims about singularities). With improved fairness (time-matched), multiple seeds, richer metrics (including localized errors), explicit noise specifications, and inclusion of basis- and loss-sensitive baselines, the work could be a valuable resource paper. In its current form, I recommend rejection, with the hope that a revised, more rigorous and comprehensive version will be submitted to a venue suitable for empirical benchmarking studies.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_084.assessment:
The paper tackles an important and timely problem and proposes a compelling shape-aware representation that reduces surface privatization to functional mean estimation with GDP. The theoretical extension of GDP to functional data via Cameron–Martin densities is sound in spirit and useful for composition. However, the empirical evaluation, as currently presented, does not convincingly support the core utility claims: (i) the baseline sensitivity for means is almost certainly incorrect (range rather than range/n), materially biasing the comparison; (ii) the proposed mechanism’s sensitivity parameter τ is estimated from the private data, so the experimental releases are not DP as claimed; and (iii) comparisons are limited and metrics are not fully robust, with no ablations or private hyperparameter selection. On the theoretical side, minor inconsistencies in calibration formulas should be corrected and clarified. I believe the proposed representation and the GDP-in-FDA framing are promising and could be impactful once the privacy calibration is fixed (e.g., RKHS clipping) and the experimental comparison is strengthened with correct baselines and broader metrics. In its current form, I recommend rejection, with the hope that a revised version addresses the sensitivity calibration, includes fair baselines (including intrinsic manifold DP mechanisms), and provides more comprehensive and reproducible empirical evidence.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_046.assessment:
This paper presents a novel and promising perspective on catastrophic forgetting in LLMs by leveraging function vectors as both a diagnostic and a mitigation mechanism. The empirical evidence that FV similarity tracks forgetting and that targeted FV interventions can partially restore performance is compelling, and the proposed FVG training wraps neatly around diverse CL baselines with consistent improvements, especially for in-context ability. However, the causal claim that forgetting primarily originates from biased activation rather than parameter overwrite, while plausible and aligned with recent “pseudo forgetting” work, is not fully nailed down; additional controls, variance reporting, and ablations (S/layer selection, random-vector interventions, compute overhead) are needed to solidify the conclusions and to assess practicality at scale. Overall, I find the core idea original and valuable to the ICLR community, with strong potential impact; I recommend acceptance if the authors can strengthen the experimental rigor and positioning relative to closely related pseudo-forgetting and intervention-vector literature.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_090.assessment:
This paper introduces a practical, well-motivated technique to reduce activation memory by approximately a quarter for common transformer architectures with negligible overhead and no observed accuracy loss in the presented experiments. The idea—store the nonlinearity output plus a 1-bit branch mask and approximate f'∘f^{-1}(y)—is simple yet effective, and the engineering (bit-packing, Triton kernels) appears mature. The empirical validation is encouraging but somewhat narrow: it would benefit from broader tasks/metrics, end-to-end peak memory reporting, and comparisons to standard baselines like checkpointing and ACTNN. The approximations are fitted rather than theoretically analyzed; while this is acceptable for an engineering contribution, some error characterization would strengthen confidence. Overall, I find the contribution original enough, useful in practice, and likely to see adoption as an easy win for memory-constrained training. I lean toward acceptance after addressing the requested clarifications and expanding the evaluation, especially with end-to-end memory numbers and stronger baselines.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_042.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and timely problem—service-level imperceptibility of LLM watermarks—and presents a conceptually simple, unified, and empirically strong black-box identification method. The core idea of leveraging cross-prompt consistency under fixed keys, made operational via clever prompt constructions, is novel and broadly applicable to many “during generation” watermark families. The empirical coverage is impressive and the defense proposal (Water-Bag) is concrete and well-analyzed, making the work valuable for both researchers and practitioners. The main reservations concern practicality and calibration: the method is sample-heavy, depends on assumptions about key stability that providers can circumvent, and relies on heuristic statistical calibration. Additionally, comparisons to the most relevant prior black-box method and broader decoding settings are limited, and the theoretical support is high level. Despite these caveats, the contribution is substantive and likely to influence how watermarks are designed and deployed. I lean toward acceptance, contingent on strengthened calibration, more realistic deployment tests, and a direct comparison with concurrent black-box detection baselines.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_048.assessment:
This paper addresses an important and timely problem—preference-data poisoning during alignment—and delivers a useful, reasonably comprehensive benchmark with several nontrivial empirical insights. The breadth of models, the standardized threat model, and the analyses of scaling, poison ratios, and trigger generalization make this a valuable resource. The main limitations are methodological (dependence on a single reward model for deterioration evaluation, limited human/multi-judge validation), scope (primary focus on DPO rather than RLHF), and framing (claims about “deceptive alignment” that outpace the evidence). Nonetheless, the benchmark, datasets, and results are likely to be impactful for the ICLR community, especially for researchers developing defenses in RLHF pipelines. I recommend acceptance after tempering claims around deceptive alignment, adding variance/reproducibility details, and (where feasible) triangulating alignment-deterioration metrics with an additional evaluator or limited human assessment.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_060.assessment:
This paper presents a well-motivated and carefully executed meta-learning approach for learning from multiple noisy annotators in few-shot settings. The key idea—meta-learning embeddings while adapting via a differentiable EM algorithm on a simple but effective crowdsourcing model and simulating annotators during meta-training—translates into strong empirical gains and an efficient training procedure. The work is technically sound, clearly written, and demonstrates practical value on both synthetic and real multi-annotator data. The main limitations are the instance-independent annotator model, modest exploration of annotation sparsity, and limited sensitivity analyses for the pseudo-annotator prior. Despite these, the contribution is significant and timely for ICLR, and the proposed framework should be of broad interest to researchers working on robust few-shot learning, weak supervision, and crowd modeling. I recommend acceptance.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_002.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and timely problem: building a general-purpose, probabilistic, diffusion-transformer for time series that unifies multiple tasks and allows finetuning-free physics-informed decoding. The unified mask design and transformer conditioning are sensible and empirically useful, and the breadth of tasks covered is commendable. However, the technical novelty is moderate—many architectural and training elements are known—and the physics-informed component, while well motivated, is presented with rudimentary theory and without comparisons to several highly relevant, recent physics-constrained diffusion approaches. The experimental claims would be much stronger with complete pretraining details, strict overlap controls, calibration analyses, runtime reporting, and deeper baselines on anomaly detection and physics tasks. Overall, I find the direction promising and the system potentially impactful, but I recommend rejection in its current form due to insufficient methodological transparency and missing key comparisons; with these issues addressed, the work could become a strong contribution for the community.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_014.assessment:
This paper addresses an important and timely challenge: resolving domain shift to enable pooled training across non-invasive MEG datasets for speech decoding. The application of adversarial harmonization to MEG appears novel, and the Brainmagick results convincingly show that pooling with adversarial harmonization yields consistent and statistically significant improvements over controls, supported by diagnostics that the encoder unlearns dataset-identifying features. The MEGalodon results are more mixed, highlighting both the promise and the instability of adversarial harmonization and the nontrivial impact of demographic confounds, especially age. While the contributions are meaningful and the open-source implementation is valuable, the experimental evidence is not yet fully comprehensive: cross-dataset transfer to held-out corpora is missing, baselines to non-adversarial alignment methods are limited, and the MEGalodon downstream gains are inconsistent. Overall, I view this as a solid and thought-provoking step toward MEG harmonization with clear signs of promise, but I recommend a weak reject at this stage due to the need for stronger, broader validation and additional baselines. With more rigorous cross-dataset evaluations, stability improvements, and comparative analyses, this work could have significant impact.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_008.assessment:
This paper addresses an important and practical problem—improving retrieval by enriching document representations with LLM-generated signals—and demonstrates substantial empirical gains across popular retriever architectures. The approach is simple, model-agnostic, and likely impactful for practitioners. However, the paper’s core technical derivation contains a significant flaw (the linearity of cosine similarity and the reduction to Eq. (3)), and the treatment of encoder space consistency for DRAGON is unclear. The experimental section, while positive, does not engage rigorously with standard metrics, full-benchmark coverage, efficiency analyses, or strong and closely related baselines (corpus-side expansion with docTTTTTquery, PromptReps, DRAMA). The training refinements are not validated publicly. As a result, despite promising results, the contribution currently falls short of the rigor expected for ICLR. I encourage the authors to correct the technical derivations, add comprehensive and standard evaluations with efficiency metrics and strong baselines, and publicly validate the training techniques. With these improvements, the work could become a strong and influential systems paper.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_078.assessment:
This paper presents a timely and practically useful contribution: a mini-batch kernel k-means algorithm with a principled truncation scheme, sublinear per-iteration time in n, and a dimension-free early-stopping guarantee via γ. The technical development is solid, adapting recent advances in mini-batch analysis to Hilbert spaces and providing clear bounds on truncation error and termination time. Empirically, the method achieves substantial speedups with minimal loss in quality on several datasets, and the comparison of learning-rate schedules is a valuable add-on.
The main weaknesses lie in experimental scope and one inconsistency. Not benchmarking against Nyström/RF-based kernel k-means baselines leaves a gap in understanding the method’s position among scalable kernel clustering approaches. Additionally, the log vs. log^2 batch-size requirement should be clarified and corrected. Some presentation polish (typos, more explicit truncated-update pseudocode) would also help.
On balance, the paper advances a relevant problem with a method that is both theoretically principled and practically impactful. With minor corrections and broader empirical comparisons, it would be a strong addition to ICLR. I lean toward acceptance.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_020.assessment:
This paper presents a practical and thoughtfully designed approach to tabular imputation and generation that leverages the strengths of gradient-boosted trees while avoiding diffusion’s train–inference gap for conditional tasks. The UnmaskingTrees formulation is simple yet powerful, and BaltoBot is a neat hierarchical device that provides efficient probabilistic prediction, fast sampling, and closed-form densities—features that are nontrivial to obtain simultaneously. Empirically, the method is competitive or superior to state-of-the-art baselines where it matters most for small, incomplete tabular datasets. The paper is well-motivated, provides code, and is accessible to practitioners.
The main areas to improve before publication are (a) clarifying and unifying the complexity analysis, (b) adding robustness and calibration diagnostics (especially for BaltoBot), and (c) deepening the discussion (and if possible, evidence) about scalability and parameter sensitivity. Despite these, the contribution is substantive and timely, offering a credible alternative to diffusion-based tabular generative models for conditional settings. I lean toward acceptance at ICLR, with the expectation that the authors can address the noted clarifications and add minimal but impactful diagnostics.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_044.assessment:
This paper presents an appealing and practically motivated reframing of counterfactual recourse from feature-level edits to high-level, real-world-like actions, and complements single-agent optimization with amortized, data-driven generators trained on agent–CFE pairs. The framing is clear, the optimization formulations are straightforward, and the empirical accuracies of the learned generators are promising. However, significant concerns limit the current submission’s strength: the realism and feasibility of hl-continuous actions (notably the use of signed “food” vectors) are unclear; the hl-discrete settings rely on synthetic actions; hl-id is evaluated as a re-labeling abstraction; and key empirical comparisons to modern global/amortized or causal-aware baselines are missing. The fairness and efficiency claims would be more convincing with standard metrics, stronger baselines, validity/cost optimality verification for learned generators, and realistic domain constraints.
Overall, I view this as an interesting direction with solid potential, but the methodological gaps and empirical omissions make it borderline for ICLR. Strengthening the action realism and constraints, adding robust comparisons (PAR, CFN, trees), verifying validity/optimality at inference, and expanding fairness and runtime analyses would substantially improve the paper and its impact.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_050.assessment:
This paper presents a practical and impactful recipe for training text-only LLM judges without human preference labels, achieving state-of-the-art performance among generative LLM-as-a-Judge approaches on RewardBench and matching or exceeding judges trained on labeled data. The core idea—constructing contrastive pairs via modified-instruction responses and filtering via the current judge’s correct verdicts—is simple, scalable, and empirically effective. The experiments are solid and include useful ablations, though the work would be strengthened by reporting dataset sizes/retention dynamics per iteration, running broader human-agreement evaluations, and statistically characterizing improvements. Some methodological choices (re-initialization each iteration) are not fully justified, and the pipeline still presumes access to strong base models; robustness to rubric shifts and semantic label noise deserve deeper analysis. Overall, the contribution is timely and valuable for the community given the central role of judges in training and evaluation. I lean toward acceptance, with the caveat that additional analysis on data scales, robustness, and human agreement would further solidify the claims.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_010.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and well-documented issue—position bias—using a principled, mechanistic approach that is training-free and applicable across tasks. The core idea of query-dependent bidirectional inter-document attention plus position reassignment from position-free attention scores is simple, compelling, and supported by both theory (invariance proof sketch) and strong empirical results, especially for evaluators on RewardBench reasoning. The comparisons against mechanistic baselines (PCW/SP/NIA) are thorough and favor PINE, and RAG results strikingly demonstrate position-invariant performance without sacrificing average accuracy.
That said, the theoretical claims rely on nontrivial implementation details (per-query reassignment with RoPE; application at all layers/heads), and the paper currently under-specifies these, leaving questions about practicality (KV caching, latency) and the exact conditions under which invariance holds in deployed systems. Some experiments (molecules, math) are under-detailed, and broader comparisons to related permutation-invariant architectures and judge bias-mitigation frameworks would strengthen context. Overall, the contribution is novel and significant with clear practical value; addressing the implementation clarifications, reporting statistical significance, and expanding ablations would elevate it to a strong ICLR paper.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_062.assessment:
This paper addresses an important and timely problem—how to tightly couple learned component surrogates with mechanistic simulators in a way that preserves constraints and solver efficiency. The implicit gray-box formulation and the practical integration of DNN Jacobians into Newton–Raphson are well-motivated and demonstrated on representative power-system tasks, with encouraging evidence for sensitivity fidelity, constraint satisfaction, and modest runtime reductions. However, the methodological core (AD-based Jacobian assembly for implicit solves) is closely aligned with established differentiable programming literature, and the paper under-positions itself relative to recent neural DAE/PH frameworks and AD-enabled implicit solvers. The current experimental baselines (e.g., PQ surrogate) are relatively weak, scalability evidence is limited, and a key implementation detail (Algorithm 1) appears incorrect or at least under-specified for computing the required Jacobians. Overall, I view the contribution as promising and practically relevant but not yet at the level of rigor, comparative evaluation, and clarity expected for ICLR acceptance. Strengthening the Jacobian computation details, adding stronger baselines and scaling studies, and positioning the work within the broader differentiable simulation literature would significantly improve the paper.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_056.assessment:
This paper makes a valuable and timely empirical case that data valuation is brittle to routine preprocessing choices and can induce harmful technical and ethical side effects (e.g., worsened class imbalance, potential undervaluation of minority groups). The proposed DValCards framework is a constructive and pragmatic response that could meaningfully improve transparency and responsible use of DV in practice. However, the work currently reads more as a cautionary empirical study and advocacy piece than as a technically deep ICLR contribution. The fairness analysis is underdeveloped in the main text, algorithmic remedies are limited, and comparisons to stability-oriented DV methods are missing. Strengthening the empirical coverage (especially fairness), adding at least one stabilizing baseline, and providing a worked DValCard example or user study would considerably raise the paper’s impact and suitability for ICLR. As it stands, I lean toward a borderline reject for ICLR, while acknowledging the importance and practical relevance of the message; with the suggested additions, the paper could be a strong candidate either here or at venues focused on responsible AI and transparency.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_096.assessment:
The paper tackles an important problem—learning informative, adaptive rewards for imitation in high-dimensional control—and presents an appealing trainer–student perspective with solid empirical breadth. However, the core mechanism, as currently specified, appears to boil down to training a pointwise reward head to match the discriminator output, which is conceptually very close to standard AIL with a different reward transform, and the paper does not convincingly demonstrate additional long-horizon or curriculum benefits attributable to the trainer beyond what an evolving discriminator already provides. The sign inconsistency in the trainer reward is a serious technical issue that must be rectified. Stronger ablations (especially a direct non-trainer mapping from D to student reward), clearer treatment of state-only demonstrations, and comparisons to modern stabilized IRL/IL baselines are needed to substantiate the claimed advantages.
Given these concerns, I lean toward rejection in the current form. With corrected equations, a rigorous ablation showing that the trainer materially outperforms direct discriminator-based rewards under identical optimizers, and expanded related-work baselines, this work could become a valuable contribution, especially if it can demonstrate genuine long-horizon shaping beyond pointwise discriminator matching.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_080.assessment:
This is a compelling and practically appealing idea: a one-line, post-training operator that consistently increases robustness on strong adversarially trained models with negligible clean-accuracy cost and minimal overhead. The breadth of empirical results and ablations is a notable strength, and if the reported gains withstand stronger adaptive evaluation, the contribution would be impactful for both research and practice.
However, the current evaluation leaves open a critical concern: the defense is non-differentiable and explicitly masks gradients around μch, yet there is no BPDA/EOT-based white-box assessment on the marquee models. The authors acknowledge vulnerability to straight-through gradients in the Limitations, which directly targets the central risk of gradient obfuscation. Additionally, the α-selection procedure appears to rely on test-set performance, which can inflate claims. Given ICLR’s standards for robustness work, I cannot recommend acceptance without rigorous adaptive evaluation (BPDA/EOT, stronger recent single attacks like PMA, targeted/multi-targeted settings) and a clean validation protocol for hyperparameter tuning. I view this as a strong, promising submission that could become a valuable contribution with these gaps addressed.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_074.assessment:
SuperMark presents a simple yet effective training-free post-processing watermarking framework that smartly unifies noise-space embedding with image-conditioned diffusion SR and DDIM inversion. The idea is elegant and practically significant: it eliminates both heavy model training and per-image optimization while delivering strong robustness against many common and some adaptive attacks, with reasonable fidelity and broad transferability. The empirical study is substantial and the writing is clear.
At the same time, two aspects limit the strength of the current submission: (i) the robustness characterization does not include stochastic resampling/trajectory-deflection attacks known to be effective against inversion-based schemes, and (ii) practical aspects (runtime/memory) and fairness controls (capacity/strength matching across baselines) are missing. Additionally, a more explicit treatment of model-mismatch scenarios and geometric/composite perturbations would bolster claims of general robustness.
On balance, I find the contribution novel and valuable for the community. With added experiments on stochastic samplers, runtime reporting, and tighter baseline controls, this work would be a strong fit for ICLR. As it stands, I lean toward acceptance, given the clean idea, training-free nature, breadth of evaluation, and practical importance, while encouraging the authors to address the noted gaps.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_086.assessment:
The paper tackles an important and timely question—scalable internal evaluation of LLMs—by proposing a simple, fast surrogate for nuclear norm based on the L1,2 norm. The computational contribution is clear and practically relevant; the reported speedups over matrix entropy are compelling. However, the central scientific claim—that the proposed metric is a reliable evaluator and ranker of model performance—remains weakly supported. The approximation lacks theoretical error guarantees, the implemented score is not the nuclear norm but an upper-bounding proxy, key hyperparameters and layer choices are under-specified and unablated, and, most importantly, there is no rigorous correlation analysis with external quality signals (human preference/benchmarks). The observed anomalies and cross-family inconsistencies further suggest the metric needs calibration and deeper study before being used for model ranking.
Overall, I view the work as a promising step towards faster internal diagnostics but not yet ready for a top-tier venue without substantially stronger theoretical and empirical validation. I encourage the authors to (i) quantify approximation error to the true nuclear norm, (ii) add comprehensive correlation studies against Arena Elo and downstream tasks, (iii) ablate D, layer choice, and normalization schemes, and (iv) position the method among recent internal-evaluation works (entropy/MNN hybrids, layer-wise metrics) and SVD-free spectral estimators. With these additions, the paper could make a solid, impactful contribution.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_034.assessment:
This paper presents a compellingly simple and effective idea: leverage Neural Collapse to impose a clear feature-space separation between ID and OOD during auxiliary-OOD fine-tuning. The orthogonality-based separation loss is easy to implement, complements standard OE objectives, and yields strong empirical gains across canonical OOD benchmarks with multiple architectures. The work is well-motivated and includes informative ablations and geometric diagnostics. The main reservations are (i) reliance on NC and feature dimensionality without theoretical guarantees, (ii) incomplete baseline coverage (notably DAC/K+1) and partial fairness concerns (auxiliary OOD contamination risks; score tailoring), and (iii) limited discussion versus recent auxiliary-OOD advances that also target representation-level improvements. Addressing these concerns—especially adding DAC comparisons, reporting MSP-only results in main tables, and clarifying normalization/training details—would materially strengthen the case. Overall, I find the contribution valuable and practically impactful; with the suggested additions and clarifications, it would be a solid fit for ICLR.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_032.assessment:
The paper tackles an important and well-motivated challenge—reducing communication for nonlinear layers in private Transformer inference—and proposes a provocative idea: unify nonlinearities to inverse square root and eliminate initial-guess communication via a double approximation enabled by share flooding. The empirical gains reported over Iron/CrypTen are promising, and the unification perspective is appealing.
However, I have significant concerns about technical soundness and completeness. The derivations in Section 3.2 contain ambiguities and apparent errors (handling of logs, signs, and floating-point decomposition for negative shares), and there is no formal error or security analysis of the double approximation and flooding approach. On the empirical side, the evaluation omits the most relevant recent baselines (East, Nimbus, SecFormer), limiting the ability to judge SOTA impact. The accuracy validation is confined to a subset of GLUE with limited ablations beyond iteration counts and an empirical exponent “closeness” study.
In its current form, I do not believe the paper meets the bar for ICLR. Strengthening the theoretical foundations (rigorous derivation, error bounds, and a formal security argument), expanding the evaluation to include modern baselines and broader tasks, and clarifying implementation details would substantially improve the work. The core idea is interesting and potentially impactful, but it requires a more careful and complete development.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_026.assessment:
This paper tackles an important practical problem—instability and loss spikes in large-model training—and proposes a simple, well-motivated optimizer that blends sign-like robustness with bounded per-coordinate updates and Nesterov-style acceleration without memory overhead. The analytical results are sound in spirit (deriving explicit bounds for Adam’s update, ensuring S3’s updates are capped, and establishing an O(T^{-1/4}) nonconvex rate), though not groundbreaking and with some assumptions requiring clarification. Empirically, the reported gains on ImageNet and GPT-2 (345M) are promising, and the preliminary 7B curves suggest potential. However, the experimental section has gaps: missing key baselines (Sophia, AdaFactor, LAMB), limited long-run LLM results, unclear fairness in hyperparameter searches, and the need for stronger causal evidence that per-coordinate cap is the dominant factor in spike mitigation across settings.
Overall, I find the optimizer idea appealing and practically relevant, but I recommend a weak reject at this stage due to the breadth and rigor gaps in the empirical evaluation and some overstatements in claims. With additional comprehensive baselines (especially for LLMs), longer training runs, clearer fairness protocols, and improved theoretical exposition (clarifying assumptions), this work could be a strong contender for publication.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_092.assessment:
This paper presents an elegant and potentially impactful theoretical advance: a provable almost-linear-time algorithm for RoPE attention under bounded-entry assumptions, achieved by a novel combination of the polynomial method and FFTs over a newly highlighted matrix class (rescaled Toeplitz). The lower bound under SETH aligns with and extends prior hardness narratives, indicating tightness of the tractable regime. Conceptually, the work is well motivated: RoPE breaks low-rank structure but introduces Toeplitz/Hadamard structure that is indeed FFT-friendly, and the paper formalizes how to leverage this.
However, the current presentation leaves a crucial gap: the overall runtime depends on the number of monomials in the polynomial expansion, which, in the worst-case combinatorial accounting, appears polynomial in n when d = Θ(log n), potentially contradicting the n^{1+o(1)} claim. The authors state that this sum is n^{o(1)} “later,” but the provided text does not include the necessary derivation. Additionally, error handling (domain bounds for exp, negative inputs, and normalization) and end-to-end complexity statements need to be clarified, and the writing quality should be improved to meet a top-tier venue’s standards. I believe the core idea is strong and original, and with the requested clarifications and a tightened complexity analysis, this work could merit acceptance. In its current form, I lean toward a weak reject, primarily due to the missing piece on bounding the expansion size and the lack of a consolidated, rigorous runtime/error analysis.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_098.assessment:
The paper tackles an important and challenging problem: enriching weak feature spaces in high-stakes domains with interpretable latent features. Conceptually, casting latent feature mining as text-to-text propositional reasoning and operationalizing it with a four-step LLM pipeline is appealing and practically relevant. The empirical results show promising, sometimes large, gains in two distinct domains, suggesting that the method can be impactful.
However, for a top-tier venue, the paper needs stronger methodological controls and broader comparisons. Key gaps include: (i) rigorous leakage checks and clearer data/protocol descriptions; (ii) comparisons to strong representation-learning and modern tabular baselines; (iii) robustness, calibration, and subgroup-fairness analyses; and (iv) clearer evidence of CoT faithfulness and the specific contribution of external context versus re-encoding of X. The reliance on closed models for best performance also weakens reproducibility. With these additions, the work could become a valuable contribution; in its current form, I view it as promising but not yet fully ready for ICLR. I encourage the authors to address the concerns above, as the core idea and application space are compelling.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_038.assessment:
This paper addresses an important gap by elevating 3D visual grounding from single-step referrals to multi-step, task-oriented scenarios, and backs it with a significant dataset (SG3D) and an embodied subset (SG3D-Nav). The proposed SG-LLM, though architecturally modest, is well-motivated and empirically effective, and the benchmarking is broad and informative. The main limitations lie in component ablations, incomplete baseline coverage across all splits, simplified grounding via GT masks, and limited exploration of memory design and long-horizon robustness. Despite these gaps, the work substantially advances problem formulation, resources, and empirical understanding. I lean toward acceptance given the clear problem importance, dataset contribution, and solid empirical insights, contingent on adding key ablations/analyses and clarifying dataset verification and baseline coverage.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_068.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and timely problem—learning stochastic process priors that support tractable densities and Bayesian regression—by extending flow matching to function spaces with a neural-operator backbone. The conceptual unification of prior learning and posterior sampling, the OT-inspired conditional path, and the deployment of divergence-based likelihoods for arbitrary point sets are compelling. The GP and truncated-GP experiments are convincing, and the qualitative results on complex 2D fields are promising. However, several aspects require strengthening before publication at ICLR: (i) formal guarantees or at least clearer arguments for cross-set consistency (a prerequisite for a bona fide stochastic process), (ii) correction and clarification of the divergence estimator and practical likelihood computation, (iii) quantitative evaluation on the non-GP benchmarks and broader baselines including recent function-space diffusion/conditioning methods, and (iv) a more explicit account of computational costs and architectural choices for arbitrary point sets. With these additions and clarifications, OFM could be a valuable contribution bridging operator learning and probabilistic modeling of functions. As it stands, I lean toward a weak accept contingent on addressing the technical and empirical gaps noted above.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review
